speaker: interviewer
text: It's 1995, survivor Regina Gelb. Interviewer's name is Chana Meier.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whether we went with the beginning of the evacuation or the end, or the middle, I couldn't say. All I know is that it was a snowy winter, and we were being driven day and night. And as I'm told by my sisters, and I know it for a fact, I fell asleep, completely and totally. I could not manage that march. And they just dragged me. They just absolutely dragged me. And, and-- I had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] complete-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had hallucinations that did not measure up to reality. You see, I thought I was somewhere else. And, and you see, there was this winter road, covered with snow, and the trees on both sides of the road. And it looked to me like an enclosure. And I just thought it was-- whatever it was, I absolutely was cut off from what was happening. And what was happening was, was just dreadful. Because the death march was a death march. It was a march where people were falling by the wayside from exhaustion, from lack of sleep, from hunger, from just not being able to do it anymore and being hit by the-- by a butt of, of the gun, of the rifle, or shot. Again, and with the dogs, the whole-- it was like, like the same tape, you know, that repeated. Except this is now dead of winter, dark and snowy. And again, we don't know where we're going. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't even remember how many days that lasted, truly. Because I really slept through that march. They dragged me. And we finally one night, I think we were stopped at the barn, or some such big enclosure. And they were rushing us in and rushing us in. And I-- my sisters were pulling me in. I couldn't move fast enough. I got hit with a rifle. And I fell right at the doorstep to that barn. And of course, I was dead asleep anyhow. But next day, when the commotion started, I was at the door. So when I woke up, I stood at the door. And I found my sisters again, you see. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any hope of surviving at that point? Did you have plans? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we had absolutely no plans. For the reason that, first of all, no one experience equaled the other experience. So you could not draw on your experiences. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think that we, the three of us, were all that clever, you know. There are some people are very clever about living. They have an onion and exchange it for cigarettes and exchange the cigarettes for whatever, and then they end up with a piece of bread. We never could manage things. I guess, we grew up in another setting. And we went just being carried, I guess, by the-- by life. We were just being-- not that we were passive, but certainly we couldn't be active. And as far as plans are concerned, if there is anything in life you learn from experience, it's how to use your previous experience to your future experience. But in this string of experiences, beginning with the first day of the war, there was absolutely nothing that you could have predicted or foreseen or otherwise applied to plan for anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: By now we knew that we are slated for destruction. The question was, how long would that take. How long and how many other things are going to come around to decide our fate?

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, right along, I knew that my fate would be decided immediately, if I were to be left alone. That was no question in mind. Plans or no plans or experiences, or whatever. If I were to be alone, I would not survive, I knew it. And, and, and that's how it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my passivity was really my complete faith in my two sisters-- their judgment, their love, and, and their protection. And, and of course, we supported each other, you see. Because we were, we were really, in all-- as they say, in the same boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the support that we gave each other-- and we still had these other people around us, you see-- that sustained us. We still were running into these other people, here and there. That, that at least, that was a thread from the old life. So maybe you weren't such an absolute reject from life because there was, after all, somebody there who know you, who knew you as that other person in the other life, which was life, and this being not just torture, but some kind of inhuman, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know-- imagination couldn't paint this. Well anyhow, as I said, I was hit, and I was-- I was-- I fell right by the doorstep to the big barn. And when they came out, we found each other. And we walked, I don't remember how long. That was the most-- another horrendous experience, because it was so cold, and we didn't-- we couldn't relieve ourselves, and there was no food. Finally, we, we came near a siding, railroad siding, whether it was a station or a siding or whatever. And there were cattle cars lined up on the railroad, open cattle cars. I think they're called coal cars, coal cars or something. And this is now snow is falling. And we were shoved into these cars. The snow was falling, at least we put-- we ate the snow off our clothes, so that, that sustained us, you know, which we did right on the death march as well. And then the train started moving somewhere again, you see. And nobody told us, what, how, when, or whatever. And now we were maybe with one or two or three of the old group. Because you know, in this whole [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] mix of people from everywhere, a lot of them got killed on the road, and a lot of them, whatever. Anyhow, we rode that train. And I don't know, again, on-- for how many days, until we came to a station, and it said Ravensbruck. Never heard of that, never knew what it was, what it meant. But of course, now I know that this was another concentration camp inside of Germany, meaning west, actually northwest of Auschwitz, but into Germany proper. And Ravensbruck, as I understand it, was once a concentration camp with a designation of whatever number of people-- 10,000, 15,000, whatever. By the time we had arrived in Ravensbruck, all these other camps were being emptied. So the capacity of that camp was-- to say that, that it was over capacity is an understatement. They had to put out big tents to accommodate us. And whatever horror there was in Auschwitz, this was double horror. Because here there was nothing, absolutely nothing. [POINTING] There was no order. There were people on top of people. In fact what happened, we were sleeping in a-- one of these tents. And my sister Anna took off her shoes and put it next to her head. And we slept right next to each other, because this wasn't even on bunks, I don't think. It was just lined up on the floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: A woman came and took the shoes. She said, these are my shoes. And my sister says, what do you mean? These are-- these are my shoes. They're my pair. Well, I can tell you that when the other women around us saw what was happening, they were beside themselves. They got up and say how dare you, where do you come from to take this? There was a real fight. And of course, my sister, Anna, is also called Hania, she would never have gotten those boots-- those shoes back. That was absolutely impossible.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, the people that were around us helped-- helped her fight off the imposter, whoever she was. She was another poor soul who probably didn't have shoes. And she decided-- nobody took off the shoes, because I mean, Hania had such bad feet, she decided she has to take off her shoes. If you took off your shoes, you wouldn't have them. If you took off your hat, you wouldn't have it. There was no food. You know, in Auschwitz, they gave you this, this horrible stuff to drink or to eat, whatever it was, which was the watery coffee and turnip with sand for soup. But you know that when you came back and they counted you and you had that soup, that was that soup, whatever. Here, there was no time, place, or anything. No place to sleep, not even nine to a-- to a bunk. Because there was no organization. There was, there was such-- the influx of, of, of all these people from all over. The camp couldn't contain it. It was absolutely--

speaker: interviewee
text: No one was working.

speaker: interviewee
text: No one was working. Nobody was even accommodated. In fact, when we came out of the train, we were told to stay on the snow where we were. Because they couldn't even march us into the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember distinctly when we got-- when we got up, everybody was sitting down. And after they called us, which was probably next morning, or whatever, when we left, that whole area where we sat was covered with feces and urine and everything. And we were just sent in. And then there was more people sent in. Because the death march was one way of sending people in. And then there were other camps that were more east, that were also being emptied into Ravensbruck. And we met, in fact, we met one lady, my mother's friend, who perished there. I mean, we met her, you know, we came across her in Ravensbruck, in all this-- she never made it out of that camp. As I know, a lot of older women didn't, because that was-- [SIGHS] again, I don't know numbers. I couldn't tell you. But, but the-- it was just not bursting at the seams, but it was just-- it was just people on top of people. And there was humanity just waiting for whatever. No work and no food, and no nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that the war was at an end?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, we had no idea. Again-- again, we were in the eye of the storm. And you see how much further into Germany we were in, now.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were very much on the German territory proper, you see? We were out of Poland by now. We had no idea. We just knew that-- that there's something what is happening. But why this is happening, we wouldn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, the situation in Ravensbruck was so absolutely horrendous and impossible that they called for volunteers to leave. Whoever wants to go, there were trucks lined up, volunteers to go out. And my sisters decided that we better get out, wherever. Even though we were quite aware of the fact that going on a truck could very well mean to extinction. Because that is now-- that was the pattern-- that if you were somewhere and then you were sent away somewhere, you probably could end up in some chimney or, or shot in the woods, in the forest. But staying in Ravensbruck was absolutely impossible at that point. And we are now talking about, well, end of January, February, OK, 1945. So we decided-- not we-- my sisters decided that we will volunteer for those-- to get out. And we got loaded onto the trucks with some other women. And we drove, I don't know how far or how long, I don't know. And then we came, and we came to another camp, a small, empty camp. Empty. It had barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that name-- the name of that camp was Retzow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course we didn't know what it was or how it was. But later on we found out that Retzow was basically a camp for flyers, for German flyers. Because there was an airbase not far from there, you see. And they decided, the Germans decided to use this, this transport that they brought on the-- on the-- that we came on, on the trucks-- to staff those empty barracks. So there was absolutely nothing there. There were no people there. We came in, each one of us got an assignment to a job. My sister Anna was given the job to clean the offices. It was run by German SS women. They lived in some of the barracks. And the rest of the barracks were empty. We didn't know other people were coming or not. Meanwhile, those of us who came to that empty camp were assigned the jobs. I became a maid to one of the SS women. You know, they had rooms for themselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had to carry wood and do her laundry, and, and clean her place. And of course, she never would come near me. But again, she took pity.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was really horrible to watch with other people. She used leave me little crusts of bread. She asked me once if I had enough to eat. I said no, so she leave me-- she left me crusts of bread, which she didn't eat crust. But she would never come near me, you see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister, Hania-- Anna-- had a job cleaning an SS woman's office. And my sister, Krys, has another job. Anyhow, this German woman said to me one day, she said to me, is it all right with you? I mean, you have enough to eat, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said yes, I'm fine, I'm fine. But my sister doesn't have a good job, my sister, Krys. Well, she sent her to work in the kitchen. And you see, because we were physically in-- in touch with these Germans, they did not want us to mix with the in-- prisoners. Now Retzow was being filled with other people who were to dig ditches, anti-aircraft ditches, whatever. But the people who worked for the Germans, personally, had to be separated. Because I mean, if we had lice-- I mean a woman like this, this SS woman, she probably would spit on me if she ever-- or kill me if she ever saw lice on me, you see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they gave us under the barracks-- where the kitchen was and all that-- they gave us a place to live. There were bunks there, but we did not-- we were not allowed to sleep in the regular barracks. Because after all, you know, if you come to clean her office and then something would you bring some sicknesses from the others, I mean, you know. So this was Retzow. And this is now February, 1945. And some people were already liberated, those who stayed behind in Poland. And for us, there was no end. And I couldn't even imagine how liberation would come. I, I couldn't even-- to put it in the most far-fetched [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] picture. You know, the bombs started falling, so we knew that something was now happening, which we're hoping will really be the end, that whether they kill us with the bombs is better than everything. Because I mean, we might yet be sent again and sent again. And who knows where we will finally end up, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: But the point is that-- you-- you asked me before if I had a plan. I not only had no plan, I had not even the imagination to conjure up a picture of how will it all come about. You know, you're a child and you read books, and there are-- you know, when people get liberated, there are parades, and the band is playing, and the flags are flying, and people are throwing flowers. It didn't fit the picture. And then I said to myself, maybe we'll just-- they will just march us out. Well, that wouldn't fit the picture. Because there has to be some shooting going on. And then maybe the bombs will fall. Whatever it was, I absolutely couldn't imagine. But the fact is, I was with my sisters. We all three lived of us in that unit underneath. The bombs were falling. They all sent us down, you know, to sit out the air raid, which we did. And we were glad of it. Because now they-- the Germans we scared, too. So you see, now when we suffered, we at least saw that they have something to worry about, too. Although, by that time, you know, if the bomb fell, we never had any idea what happened to mother. We, we didn't know about father at all. There were just the three of us. But we were together. And if the bombs were to take us, it really would have been fine, in the sense that all the horror that we went through, we certainly could not to go through again. And we had no idea if the war is at an end or in the middle or whatever it is. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, in my-- in my small child's mind was very glad to see that the German women that we served, that they were as scared as, as any of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to myself, well, maybe they'll now know what it feels like to, to live with fear every second of your life and-- and to anticipate greater horror, which is the only thing that the experience taught us, you know. That the more-- the only thing you can expect is more horror of another kind, you see. Not necessarily it's the same that you went before. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Could we stop at this point for a minute? We're almost-- we're almost done.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes? All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point, were you liberated from the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were not liberated. Because as it turns out, with the bombing and all, that camp had to be evacuated. So you see, one more evacuation. Except this is not January, and there is no snow. The month is April, 1945, end of April, 1945. We are lined up again, counted again. And those of us who were personal maids to the SS women have to carry their things, their rucksacks. We didn't take anything of our own, but we had to carry their stuff. And we were marched out of the camp somewhere, onto a road that was completely overrun with people of every kind-- of Germans, of, of German soldiers and German in uniform, out of uniform, and tanks and-- and horses, and the farmers with cows, and, and, and Polish POWs and Polish farmhands who worked the-- Polish, I meant Christian. The Christian Poles who were working the farms in-- in Germany during the war. Slave labor, also, in-- of another kind, because they lived on the places where they worked. Well, all that mixture, that whole conglomeration of people with us-- and not just us, but other camps being emptied-- all this mixed together. Of course, we were-- we were-- we had clothing that was marked with a big X, both at the front and on the back. So you could tell us a mile away. Because this was white lacquer type of paint, huge.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that from?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the way we were marked in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the mark, the, the x. So that if you looked at that road, you really didn't know who was who or-- and you didn't know which way the whole-- that humanity was moving. Because everything was at a standstill. And then all of a sudden, somebody comes over and says, hey, you know, there are-- there's a Swedish Red Cross here, and they have packages, Red Cross packages for us. First time in, in, in a-- years I've got a can of sardines. And how the Swedish Red Cross happened to be there at that time, I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, we only know one thing, and that is that President Roosevelt died. Because my sister overhead it on the radio, while she was cleaning the office. [LAUGHS] And that was April 14. But we didn't know anything other than that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could we stop for one minute? Yes? Just one minute? [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we found ourselves on that road that was so clogged with everybody, and, and, and nobody knew who was who and what was happening, and planes were flying over, and-- among the girls, the women, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that we worked with, you know, the girls who were serving the Germans, who also carried those bags for the Germans, as I did, the personal maids, was one woman, Halina. And she, she had an idea. If we separated ourselves from these German women whom we served-- if she-- she spoke German very well-- if she got dressed in one of the uniforms out of the rucksack that she carried or I carried or whoever carried, the maids-- if we separated ourselves, she could impersonate a German woman and say that we just got lost, 12 of us, we just got-- you know. Because we couldn't hide who we were, because we were painted with those, with those crosses, which is the exactly what we did. We-- people, you know, people were getting their rations. And, and everything was stuck in the way. And the German women, of course, helped themselves to the cigarettes that were being given out. And you know, they were such-- well, that's another story. They were really worse than men, in-- in many respects. They-- you could never imagine that a woman could be as cruel and brutal as these women were. Anyhow, we detached ourselves, 12 of us, altogether. And Halina was our so-called overseer. And we-- because the road was in the middle, and there were two embankments, we slid down the embankment and there were hay in the, in the field. We hid behind the hay. And we stayed there, I think, overnight. And Halina got herself dressed as an SS woman. And then meanwhile, whatever happened, the, the traffic got to move a little. And probably-- nobody would ever miss us. We knew that for sure. Nobody would miss us. And we met a Polish Christian who was employed at the farm nearby. And he said that they all ran away, that there is a villa there. And we should go and tell the woman-- there's an old woman there, or young woman, whatever-- there's a woman attendant, an attendant, to feed us. Well, we actually did what he said. We went to the house. There was the German woman. It was a villa. We thought it was fantastic. And we were going to see what happens now. We found out that there is a Russian offensive around. And that they-- on the west, from the west, there is another offensive coming. While we were in that villa, the Russians came. Meaning they arrived at the villa, and they wanted to know who was living here, and who were these people were. And we told them who we are. Halina, I think, spoke Russian also. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They said, oh great. You are here, that's good. We come tomorrow, you're going to be working for us. Meanwhile, there was a Russian woman among us, who also said-- she warned us that they rape women, and we better get out of here, which we did. The following day, we started a trek back to Poland. Because we really had no idea, now, what happened to the family, to the extended family that I told you about, Babcia Tenenblum.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you knew the war was over at this point, didn't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew because the Russians were there. We were told the war was over. But we really didn't know how the was finished, who-- who fought whom and how, and whatever. We just knew that the Russians-- we were on the German territory, inside of Germany, that was liberated by the Russians. And we started going back to Poland, in the way as, as we walked. And then we walked to a train. And then we were on the train for a day. And then we were thrown off the train because the Russians were going back. And then we sat on the, on the railroad stations. And then we got on another train, got bumped off again. Whatever it is, by the time we came back closer to Poland, we were warned by a Russian officer who turned out to be Jewish, he heard us in the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: He asked us where we were going. And we said that we were going back to Starachowice. And he said, is this a big city? And we said no, it's not a big city, it's a small town. He said, then you cannot go back, because there are all kinds of things happening in small towns to people who are returning. And so we said, well, where else are we going? Where else could we go? We have to find family.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, go to only to the big cities, Lodz or Warsaw, Krakow. So we went to Lodz. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he gave us the name of his sister, who had an apartment. And we came, there were wall to wall people, because she was taking in everybody. But the next day, we went and found out that there's a Jewish agency that registers people, returning people, whoever, Jewish people. Because there was a Red Cross that was also registering people. But the Jewish agency set up their own. And we signed our name and went there. Of course, they gave us, then, rations-- food rations. And we were-- we were in the agency every day, to look through the register, you see. And sure enough, we found Uncle Alexander, who was my father's brother from Radom. So we established a household. First we lived-- before we found uncle, we lived in a hovel. There was no electricity and nothing, just four walls that were bombed out or whatever. And when Uncle Alexander came on the scene, so to say, he helped us. He went back to his hometown. He had real property, real estate and-- he was able to get some of the money that he hid. And so when he came back, we got a very decent kind of an apartment. And the three of us lived with Uncle Alexander in Lodz. And by then it was summer, summer 1945. And subsequently, my two sisters found work in Poland. And I went to school. And I was tested and admitted to the second year of high school, and I wasn't even-- before the war I didn't even finish-- I did elementary school. But I had all this-- I knew chemistry, and I-- I mean, really. And after all the horrible experiences that I was able to even pass a test. But we stayed in Lodz. And my sister Krys met her present husband, Miles Lerman. They got married in December of the same year in Lodz. And very soon--
